content:
Hello there! I am an undergraduate student currently studying physics and mathematics at Grinnell College in Grinnell, IA. There I work as mentor at the Data Analysis and Social Inquiry Lab, participate in astrophysics research with the Physics Department, and play an active role in the student Astronomy Group. My primary research interests are in astrophysics and cosmology, and I am pursuing research opportunities in these fields. I am also an avid photographer and enjoy exploring art and design.
This personal website is intended to showcase both research projects and artistic endeavors.
summary: Hello there! I am an undergraduate student currently studying physics and mathematics at Grinnell College in Grinnell, IA. There I work as mentor at the Data Analysis and Social Inquiry Lab, participate in astrophysics research with the Physics Department, and play an active role in the student Astronomy Group. My primary research interests are in astrophysics and cosmology, and I am pursuing research opportunities in these fields. I am also an avid photographer and enjoy exploring art and design.
title: Hollis Akins

content:
The 2016 presidential election brought discussions of immigration policy and American identity to the national spotlight. Minutes into the announcement of his presidential campaign, Donald Trump launched into an attack of Mexican immigrants, referring to them as “rapists and criminals” and vowing to restrict immigration through the construction of a border wall. His first campaign ad directly targeted Muslim immigrants, tying them to “radical Islamic terrorism” and vowing to ban their entering the country. Trump promised to “make America great again,” appealing to a desire to return to the past, before the recent influx of immigrants, and his appeals were demonstrably effective in securing him the presidency.
Since inauguration, Trump’s nativist rhetoric has only become more literal, manifesting itself in his policies and actions. His travel ban on Muslim countries fulfilled his inflammatory campaign promise, and the separation of parents and children at the border serves as a brutal deterrant to immigration from Mexico and Central America. Despite this, he has managed to maintain a solid base, with his approval rating never dropping below 35%. His base appears inflamed by his xenophobic rhetoric, such as his erroneous calls for Democratic congresswomen to “go back” to where they came from, and allow it to inform other components of their political and social beliefs.
In this post, we analyze data from the November 2018 Grinnell College National Poll to examine the effects of nativism on American’s views on diversity and difference, and the role of this in the electorate.
Briefly, we find that nativism shows significant statistical relationships to views on a variety of issues, even when controlling for demographics. We find higher rates of nativism in people who express negative opinions of immigrants and refugees, as is largely to be expected. However, we also find that nativism is connected to increased perceptions of discrimination against white men and christians, and decreased perceptions of discrimination against black and Muslim Americans. We find that nativism is connected to one’s harboring of hateful feelings towards religious groups, but unconnected to one’s willingness to express said hate. Nativism is associated with beliefs on issues that don’t directly relate to immigration—the belief system is “packaged.”
Data In this analysis, we use the Grinnell College National Poll conducted November 24–27 2018 by Selzer & Co. of Des Moines, Iowa. The poll is based on telephone interviews with 1,000 U.S. adults ages 18 or older, including 828 likely voters in the 2020 general election and 769 self-identified voters in the 2018 midterm election. Results are weighted by race, age, and educational atainment to best reflect the general population based on census data.
The November 2018 GCNP includes a standard battery of questions on “American Identity,” in which respondents are asked to rate how important various traits are to “being a real American.” Some traits are based on our shared cultural and moral values, such as “to believe in treating people equally” and “to respect America’s political institutions and laws.” This is the civic conception of American Idenity. Other traits are based on religion, language, and heritage—this is the ethnic conception of American Identity. From these questions, we can assess rates of nativism—i.e. the prevalence of an ethnic, “blood & soil” conception of American identity. Figure 1 shows the responses to this battery broken down by party.
This analysis additionally utilizes questions on immigration, refugee resettlment, hate, and discrimination. The full questionaire, with summarized results, is available here.
 Analysis To assess levels of nativism among respondents, we generate a nativism index based on responses to the American Identity questions. The ethnic traits used to create the index are:
To have been born in America To have lived in America most of one’s life To be able to speak English To be a Christian  I generated the nativism index by coding “Not Important” as 0, “Just Somewhat Important” as 1, “Fairly Important” as 2, and “Very Important” as 3, summing the results from each trait, and scaling so the final index ranges from 0 to 1. Thus, a value of 1 means that the respondent answered “Very Important” to all four traits. Figure 1 above shows how the American identity battery breaks down by party, but the nativism index allows us to take into account responses other than “very important.” Notably, the mean nativism index for Republicans is 0.52, and for Democrats, 0.33.
Nativism in politics today is deeply connected to refugee resettlement, a practice the Trump administration has drastically reduced since 2016. Figure 2 shows nativism broken down by responses to the question “Currently, do you think there are too many, a lot but not too many, or not too many refugees allowed into the U.S.?” Nativism appears strongly connected to how accepting one is of refugees: a rigid, identity-based view of what it means to be an American makes one less inclined to accept migrants, even those in dire need. Recent refugees to the U.S. are predominantly from the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Burma, Ukraine, and Bhutan.
While the connection between nativism and tolerance of refugees is direct, nativist attitudes also manifest themselves in perceptions of undocumented immigrants. Figure 3 shows nativism by responses to the question “Compared to the U.S. population overall, do you think the rate of violent crime committed by [undocumented/illegal] immigrants in the United States is higher, lower, or about the same?” This question was a survey experiment—for half of respondents, the question was worded as “undocumented” immigrants. For the other half, “illegal” immigrants.
Importantly, we do not see any significant difference in nativism between those responding “Higher” to either of these questions—that is, the use of the word “illegal” in the question did not inspire more nativists to respond that immigrants commit crimes at a higher rate. The lack of this difference may suggest that nativism is not inflamed by the wording of the question, but rather rooted in a deeper xenophobic tendency.
When combining the data from both parts of the survey experiment, as in Figure 3, we can see that nativism is highly prevalent among those who say that immigrants commit crimes at a higher rate. While the connection between anti-immigrant sentiment and nativism is well-studied; however, the specific focus of this question on crime informs our understanding of nativism and one’s tolerance of diversity. Approximately 30% of respondents say that the rate of violent crime is higher among undocumented immigrants, but studies have found little-to-no evidence supporting this belief. Rather, these negative opinions are rooted in the nativist perception of these immigrants as profoundly un-American. If you adopt the ethnic conception of American identity, rather than the civic, then those who do not share your “blood & soil” would certainly not share your values and morals.
Clearly, one’s understanding of American identity regulates their views on other issues in national politics, particularly those relating to the influx of immigrants and refugees. But how, if at all, does nativism influence Americans’ acceptance of diversity with regards to other social and political issues, beyond immigration?
Figure 4 shows nativism broken down by responses to the question “Please tell me if you think [White Men], living here in the United States, experience a lot of discrimination, a little discrimination, or virtually no discrimination.” Nativism appears far more prevalent among people responding that white men face a lot or even a little discrimination, though little evidence supports this. With increasing opportunities for immigrants and minority groups, nativists see white men as the victims of a changing America with shifting priorities. They see discrimination as a zero-sum game, much in the same way they see American identity, and the resulting feeling of personal vulnerability and victimhood bleeds into other social issues.
[SEAN STOP READING HERE]
Importantly, these feelings are not limited to white male victimhood, but rather build into feelings of general racial resentment. Nativism is connected to increased belief that white men face discrimination, as well as decreased belief that African Americans and Muslims do. Figure 5 below shows the relative significance of various variables in predicting the value of percieved discrimination against white men (blue circles), African Americans (orange squares), and Muslims (green diamonds). While most control variables are not significant, only the nativism index is significant in all three regression models. The perception of white men, rather than black or Muslim Americans, as the true victims of discrimination underscores that nativism is firmly connected to racial resentment. The most extreme nativists don’t feel shared American identity across racial and religious groups, and see diversity in America as an influx of non-Americans. Racial resentment, indicated through these discrimination questions, appears deeply connected to nativism. However, religious discrimination and religious hate may also be tired to nativism, as Islamophobia remains pervasive in American society. Figure 6 shows nativism broken down by responses to the question “I’ll mention some entities you might hate, and for each I’d like you to tell me if you do—just answer yes or no” for the entity “A religion.” Nativism is more prevalent among those who say that they do hate a religion, indicating not only religious resentment but specifically hate, a extreme feeling uniquely able to regulate political beliefs.
Finally, Figure 7 shows nativism broken down by responses to the question “In general, do you feel it is okay or not okay to express feelings of hate in a public manner, including through social media?” Importantly, nativism does not appear more or less prevalent in either responses to this question, indicating an overall agreement that hate is not something to express. Though nativism and hate expression are not related, nativism and the harboring of hate for a racial group is, indicating a general feeling of discomfort expressing ………
 Conclusion From this analysis, we can tell a story as to how nativism, and its effect on Americans’ acceptance of diversity, plays a role in American politics. First, it is clear that nativism is most prevalent among people who think that the U.S. lets in too many refugees and that immigrants commit crimes at a higher rate—i.e. nativism informs and inflames xenophobic sentiment. The effect on xenophobia highlights an important result: that a nativist understanding of American identity is connected in some way to a lack of acceptance of foreigners and their culture.
Digging deeper, we find that nativism is highly prevalent among people who believe that white men/Christians experience a lot of discrimination and that African/Muslim Americans experience none. This highlights a key component of present-day American nativism: a mentality of victimization, in which nativists see themselves as the victims in a changing America, one more accepting of immigrants and diversity and placing less value on whiteness, Christianity, and heritage. As nativists are inclined towards xenophobic ideas, they see themselves as discriminated against by immigrants and minorities.
Deeper still, we see that this racial resentment and victim mentality manifests itself through feelings of hate. We see in our analysis that nativism is very prevalent among people who indicated that they hate “a religion.” While we don’t know for sure which religion this is, it is almost certainly not Christianity, and likely Islam. These feelings of hatred are driven by resentment at (this case religious) minorities, who nativists see as the actors in the discrimination against them.
Finally, we see that nativism is not related to whether one sees it as okay to express feelings of hate publicly. While these feelings of hate do in fact exist, the understanding that hate against religious or racial minorities is frowned upon reduces people’s willingness to publicly express these feelings. Thus, nativists’ feelings of racial/religious resentment and hatred are kept inside.
This brings us to our final point: how all this connects to American politics today. In 2016, Donald Trump launched a presidential campaign firmly rooted in nativist ideas, and appealing directly to these feelings of resentment and victimization. Recent major political campaigns have avoided from such overt appeals to nativist sentiment, but for Trump, this was critical to his victory. His campaign acted as an outlet for pent-up hatred and resentment in nativists, and they became the core of his political base. The data from the GCNP support this understanding of Trump’s 2016 win, and provide a route for us to further analyze these political phenomena.
summary: The 2016 presidential election brought discussions of immigration policy and American identity to the national spotlight. Minutes into the announcement of his presidential campaign, Donald Trump launched into an attack of Mexican immigrants, referring to them as “rapists and criminals” and vowing to restrict immigration through the construction of a border wall. His first campaign ad directly targeted Muslim immigrants, tying them to “radical Islamic terrorism” and vowing to ban their entering the country.
title: The Effects of Nativism on Americans' Tolerance of Diversity

content: In this project, I explored the suppression of star-formation (quenching) in dwarf satellite galaxies using ultra-high resolution cosmoligical simulations. This project was made possible by the Grinnell College Mentored Advanced Project (MAP) program, and the linked paper is the final paper for the summer project. Future presentations & publications on this topic will be listed here.
summary: Summer research project studying the evolution and star-formation properties of dwarf satellite galaxies using ultra-high resolution cosmological simulations.
title: Simulated Dwarf Galaxies

summary: Snapshots of the night sky and its many mysteries.
title: Astrophotography

summary: A series of surreal photographs created with a unique analog darkroom process.
title: Perspectives

content: In the summer of 2018, I worked as a Research Assistant at the Guilford College Cline Observatory, studying variable stars and constructing a system for automatic data analysis and collection. The project was intended primarily to facilitate future student research using the tools I developed. The data analysis pipeline is detailed in the paper below.
summary: Summer research project at the Guilford College Cline Observatory, developing a tool for programatic photometry and variable star studies.
title: Automated Data Reduction

content: For my final project for an observational astronomy class at Guilford College, I used a method called Lucky Imaging to photograph planets and incorporate them into artistic photographs. The project was exhibited as a poster presentation at the Guilford Undergraduate Symposium, and turned into an article which was published in the October 2018 edition of The Physics Teacher.
summary: Artistic planetary astrophotography using the lucky imaging technique.
title: Lucky Imaging

content: This study was completed for the Applied GIS course at Grinnell College and explored the relationship between environmental amenities, in the form of urban green space, and household income in Seattle, WA. I developed urban green space GIS layers for mean NDVI, percentage canopy cover, tree density, and percentage park at the census block group level. Using standard and geospatial regression procedures in GeoDa, I found a significant correlation between these explanatory variables and median income (R-squared = 0.43). This study could be extended to predict additional demographic variables or analyze other urban areas.
summary: GIS project exploring urban green space and environmental justice in Seattle, WA.
title: Urban Green Space

content: While not a full-fledged research project, I have used Geographic Information Systems (GIS) software to study the impacts of urban sprawl on developing cities. This was a final project for a GIS class at Guilford College, completed over three weeks in the fall of 2018. The final paper can be found here.
summary: GIS project exploring the extent of urban sprawl in Portland, OR.
title: Urban Sprawl

summary: For my final project for an observational astronomy class at Guilford College, I used a method called Lucky Imaging to photograph planets and incoorporate them into artistic photographs.
title: Imaging planets from imaginary worlds
